1: lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl — "if the LORD had not been on our side — let Israel say it now —" — the psalm opens with the liturgical invitation yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl (let Israel now say) — this is a communal confession, repeated by the assembly. The construction lûlê (if not, unless) introduces a contrary-to-fact conditional: what would have happened if YHWH had not intervened. The repeated lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû (vv1-2) creates emphasis through near-verbatim repetition.
2: lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû bĕqûm ʿālênû ʾādām — "if the LORD had not been on our side when humans rose up against us" — bĕqûm ʿālênû ʾādām (when humans/mankind rose up against us) — the enemy is called ʾādām (humanity, mortal people) — not a demonic force but human opposition. The use of ʾādām rather than a specific ethnic group generalizes the threat while simultaneously diminishing it: these are mere mortals who rose against the people of YHWH.
3: ʾăzay ḥayyîm bĕlaʿûnû bĕḥărôt ʾappām bānû — "then they would have swallowed us alive when their fury blazed against us" — bĕlaʿûnû ḥayyîm (swallowed us alive) — bālaʿ (to swallow, engulf, to be a void, to destroy; Jonah 1:17 uses the same word for the great fish). ḥayyîm (living/alive) — not merely destroyed but consumed while still alive, as Sheol is said to swallow the living in Proverbs 1:12. ḥărôt ʾappām (the burning/kindling of their nose-anger) — anger in Hebrew physiology is located in the nose (ʾap): the flaring of nostrils accompanies rage.
4: ʾăzay hammayim šĕṭāpûnû naḥlāh ʿābar ʿal-napšēnû — "then the rushing waters would have swept us away; the current would have poured over our soul/life" — the metaphor shifts from predatory animal (v3) to flood. mayim šĕṭāpûnû (the waters swept over us) — šāṭap (to overflow, sweep away, rinse, inundate). naḥlāh (the torrent, wadi-stream) — the sudden flash floods of Palestinian wadis that sweep away everything in their path. ʿal-napšēnû (over our life/soul) — the waters threatening the nepeš (the living self).
5: ʾăzay ʿābar ʿal-napšēnû hammayim hazzêdônîm — "then the raging/insolent waters would have overwhelmed us" — hammayim hazzêdônîm (the insolent/raging/proud waters) — zādôn (pride, presumptuousness, insolence, from zûd, to boil over, act presumptuously) — the same word used for human pride and arrogance (Jeremiah 50:31-32). The waters are personified as proud and insolent — as if the flood itself had the character of the proud enemies of v4:4. This is not pathetic fallacy but theological characterization: chaos and pride share the same root.
6: bārûk yhwh šellōʾ nĕtānānû ṭeref lĕšinnêhem — "blessed be the LORD, who did not give us as prey to their teeth!" — the doxology breaks the counter-factual conditional: the disasters of vv3-5 did not happen. ṭeref lĕšinnêhem (prey to their teeth) — the metaphor returns to the predatory animal image of v3 (swallowing alive). The enemies who would have swallowed them are now imagined as having teeth — a lion or wolf tearing prey. bārûk yhwh (blessed be the LORD) — the standard benediction formula for divine rescue (Genesis 14:20; 1 Samuel 25:39).
7: napšēnû kĕṣippôr nimlĕṭāh mippah yôqĕšîm happah nišbar waʾănaḥnû nimlāṭnû — "our soul escaped like a bird out of the snare of the fowlers — the snare was broken, and we escaped" — ṣippôr (a sparrow, bird) — small and vulnerable; the standard image for the weak and endangered (Psalms 11:1; Matthew 10:29-31). pah yôqĕšîm (the snare of the fowlers/trappers) — yāqôš (fowler, bird-trapper) — those who set hidden traps for birds. happah nišbar (the snare was broken) — not the bird's skill but the snare's failure; the escape is YHWH's doing, not the bird's strength.
8: ʿezrênû bĕšēm yhwh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "our help comes from the name of the LORD, the maker of heaven and earth" — the closing verse echoes Psalms 121:2 verbatim: ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth). Within the Songs of Ascents, this repetition creates an intentional bookend — the keeper of Israel (Ps 121) is the same one who breaks the snare (Ps 124). bĕšēm yhwh (in the name of the LORD) — the name as the mediating presence of YHWH, his revealed character acting on behalf of his people.
